metadata.summary: Capture screenshots (full page, element, clip, base64) and generate PDF documents from rendered HTML using Puppeteer page.screenshot() and page.pdf() APIs.

content.title: Puppeteer: Screenshot & PDF Capture
content.summary: Puppeteer can capture full-page screenshots, element-scoped screenshots, and PDF exports directly from rendered HTML. This methodology covers all screenshot options (type, quality, clip, base64) and PDF configuration (format, margins, headers/footers).

why.p: Puppeteer is the only Node-native tool that uses the same Chrome rendering pipeline as a real user's browser — screenshots and PDFs are pixel-accurate renderings. This makes it superior to HTML-to-image libraries or wkhtmltopdf for layouts that depend on CSS variables, web fonts, or JavaScript-rendered content.

list.item: Generating PDFs or screenshots from rendered HTML inside a Node-only pipeline.
Automated visual regression artifacts — capturing page state before/after a change.
Producing invoice/report PDFs from a web app's print-stylesheet layout.
Sending screenshots as evidence in monitoring/alerting workflows.
Static HTML with no JS rendering — a simpler HTML-to-PDF library (e.g. WeasyPrint for Python) avoids the Chromium dependency.
High-volume thumbnail generation at scale — a dedicated service like Puppeteer clusters or Browserless is more cost-efficient.

example.title: Full-page screenshot
Element-scoped screenshot
Screenshot with all options

rule.statement: Use type: 'jpeg' or 'webp' with quality: 80 for large pages in pipelines that store or transmit many screenshots. PNG is lossless but 3-5x larger than JPEG for photographic content.
rule.rationale: Storage and transfer costs dominate in high-volume screenshot workflows; lossy formats at quality 80 are visually indistinguishable for most content.

example.title: A4 PDF with headers and footers

rule.statement: Set printBackground: true whenever the page uses CSS background colours or images. Chromium strips backgrounds by default in PDF mode; without this flag invoices and dashboards render as black-on-white with missing visual elements.
rule.rationale: Chrome's print CSS media query hides backgrounds for paper economy; printBackground overrides this for programmatic generation where the full design is required.

antipattern.description: Calling page.pdf() immediately after page.goto() without waiting for the full render.
antipattern.reason: JavaScript-rendered content (charts, fonts, lazy-loaded images) may not have finished when goto resolves. Add waitForSelector on a key element or waitForNetworkIdle before calling pdf().
